items.title: Group-Stage Fever: The World Cup Comes Home to America
items.body:
For the first time since 1994, the men's World Cup is being played on United States soil — and this time it's bigger than anyone has ever attempted. Forty-eight teams, three host nations, and a summer that turns ordinary American cities into cauldrons of national color.

By this week, the tournament has hit its most nerve-shredding phase: the final group matches, where a single goal decides who advances and who flies home.

## The math of survival

The expanded format sends 48 teams into 12 groups of four. The top two from each group advance, along with the eight best third-place finishers. It sounds forgiving — until the last matchday, when four teams in a group can still all believe they're going through. Coaches watch two scoreboards at once. A goal in another stadium, hundreds of miles away, can flip a team's fate in an instant.

## The host-nation weight

Playing a World Cup at home is a blessing and a burden. The crowds are enormous and forgiving until they aren't; the pressure to justify the party is relentless. For the United States, Canada, and Mexico, simply reaching the knockout rounds validates a generation of investment in the sport.

## What makes the group stage special

- **No safety net.** There are no away-goal cushions, no second legs — you win, draw, or you're gone.
- **The underdog window.** Smaller nations are never more dangerous than in a one-off match with nothing to lose.
- **The unforgettable image.** Group stages are where tournaments mint their first legends — the unknown player who scores the goal a country remembers forever.

## The bigger picture

Beyond the results, something quieter is happening. Kids in cities that rarely fill a soccer stadium are watching the world's game up close, in their own time zone, in their own accent. Whatever the final standings, that's the legacy this tournament is chasing — and this is the week it starts to feel real.
